provenance.source_corpus: Partitioning the Effect of Release and Liming on Growth of Sugar Maple and American Beech Saplings

source_spans.text: Lime application had the desired effect on soil chemistry, expressed notably as a four-fold increase in calcium concentrations of the upper soil layer (0 –12 cm) and a 63% decrease in exchangeable acidity.

Lime application had the desired effect on soil chemistry, expressed notably as a four-fold increase in calcium concentrations of the upper soil layer (0 –12 cm) and a 63% decrease in exchangeable acidity.

Lime application had the desired effect on soil chemistry, expressed notably as a four-fold increase in calcium concentrations of the upper soil layer (0 –12 cm) and a 63% decrease in exchangeable acidity.
Seven years following treatments, biomass growth response of AB saplings to release was 1.5 times higher than that of SM.

nodes.name: biomass growth response of AB saplings to release

source_spans.text: Seven years following treatments, biomass growth response of AB saplings to release was 1.5 times higher than that of SM.

nodes.name: biomass growth response of SM saplings to release

source_spans.text: Seven years following treatment, released SM and AB saplings increased in biomass by 86 and 231%, respectively
In contrast, liming increased biomass growth of SM saplings (approximately 2 times compared with unlimed saplings), while there was no effect on AB growth.
Also, the availability of light and of soil Ca interacts to increase growth of SM saplings.
